---

## Figure Type

This appears to be a composite of **multiple map sheets** — specifically, a series of **point-data or well-location maps** (possibly borehole/drilling location maps or geophysical measurement point maps) at **1:200,000 scale**. The maps show dense networks of labeled points connected by lines, consistent with **seismic shot-point or gravity/magnetic observation-point maps**, or possibly **hydrogeological/drilling inventory maps**. There are at least **4–5 separate map sheet panels** visible in this composite image, arranged vertically. Each panel bears a **purple/violet rectangular stamp** (classification or approval stamp) in the upper-right corner.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The maps appear to cover portions of **map sheets M-42 and N-42** (northern Kazakhstan), consistent with the report context. The dense clustering of labeled points suggests these are **observation point location maps** for geophysical surveys (gravity, magnetic, or seismic) or possibly **water well / borehole inventory maps**. The panels appear to cover different sub-sheets of the 1:200,000 series, potentially including sheets such as:

- **M-42-I, M-42-II, M-42-VII, M-42-VIII** (northern group, upper panels)
- **N-42-XXXV, N-42-XXXVI** or adjacent sheets (lower panels)

Geographic names and settlement names appear to be labeled throughout, but at this resolution they are largely illegible. Road/path networks and administrative boundaries are visible as thin lines connecting populated points.

---

## Key Content

Each panel displays:

1. **Dense clusters of small labeled points** — these represent either geophysical observation stations (gravity, magnetic), drill holes, or hydrogeological points. The density is highest in the central and southwestern portions of each sheet, suggesting concentrated survey activity or higher settlement/infrastructure density in those zones.

2. **Linear features** — thin lines connecting points likely represent **roads, survey lines, or administrative boundaries**. Some lines appear to follow diagonal (NW-SE or NE-SW) orientations, possibly reflecting seismic profiles or tectonic lineaments.

3. **Sparse areas** — the northeastern portions of the upper panels and southeastern portions of the lower panels show fewer labeled points, suggesting lower survey density, steppe terrain, or lesser economic interest.

4. **Purple approval/classification stamps** — each panel bears a rectangular violet stamp in the upper-right quadrant, consistent with Soviet-era archival classification stamps (likely "Секретно" — Secret, or "Для служебного пользования" — For Official Use).

5. **No color differentiation** is visible — all maps appear to be **black-and-white printed or photocopied** from original drafting.

6. **Grid lines** — some panels (particularly the lower composite) show **diagonal grid lines** overlaid on the point network, consistent with a **seismic survey grid** or triangulation network.

---

## Text and Labels

Due to image resolution limitations, most text is only partially legible. The following elements can be identified or approximated:

**Purple stamps (upper right of each panel)** — appear to read something like:
> **Главное управление геодезии и картографии**
> (or similar Soviet geodetic/cartographic authority)
> **Гос. № [number]**
> **Изд. [year]**

These are standard Soviet topographic map approval stamps.

**Point labels** — hundreds of small alphanumeric labels (e.g., "скв-...", elevation values, or station numbers) are distributed across each panel, but are illegible at this resolution.

**Settlement names** — Cyrillic place names are scattered throughout; examples approximated from denser clusters might include names typical of northern Kazakhstan (e.g., villages, farms — "аул", "совхоз").

**Sheet border annotations** — coordinate values appear along the margins (latitude/longitude tick marks), consistent with standard Soviet topographic sheet formatting.

**Lower composite panel** — shows what appears to be a **title block or legend area** in the lower-left, partially obscured.

## Research Value

This composite of map sheets provides a **spatial inventory of all survey observation points or boreholes** across the M-42/N-42 study area, allowing a geologist or AI research assistant to assess **survey coverage density, distribution of data points, and any spatial gaps** in the regional geophysical or drilling dataset. The diagonal grid visible in the lower panels likely defines the **seismic or gravity survey line network**, which is critical for understanding how subsurface structural interpretations were constrained geometrically. Together, these maps answer the question: *"Where exactly was data collected, and how uniformly does it cover the region?"* — fundamental for evaluating the reliability of any subsequent mineral potential or structural assessments derived from the report.
